coredata.dc:title: Mixture of Rubia cordifolia and KI as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for steel in 3.5% NaCl: electrochemical and surface studies of steel
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Anjali
Saxena, Akhil
Kaur, Jasdeep
Rambabu, Gottipati Venkata
C, Rakesh
Lakshmi, A. Anitha
Sharma, Ankit
Kumar, Ashish
Tyagi, Rashi
coredata.dc:description: This study evaluates the corrosion inhibition performance of Rubia cordifolia extract on mild steel in a 3.5 % NaCl solution, with and without the addition of potassium iodide (SSKI oral solution). The extract contains active phytoconstituents such as alizarin, mollugin, naphthoquinone, ruberythric acid, saponins, and triterpenoids, which contribute to its anticorrosive behavior through adsorption on the steel surface. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) results revealed that both anodic and cathodic reactions were effectively suppressed, achieving maximum inhibition efficiencies of 88 % without KI and 95.2 % with KI at 400 mg/L concentration. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirmed this trend, showing an increase in charge transfer resistance (Rct) from 15.78 Ω·cm² (blank) to 139.21 Ω·cm² (without KI) and 260.03 Ω·cm² (with KI), indicating the formation of a compact and stable protective film. UV–Visible analysis further validated the adsorption of phytochemical components on the steel surface, as reflected by decreased absorbance after corrosion testing. These results demonstrate that Rubia cordifolia extract acts as an efficient, eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for steel in saline environments, and its synergistic combination with KI enhances inhibition performance through improved film stability and surface coverage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Corrosion rate
Rubia cordifolia
Corrosion efficiency
Computational studies

Corrosion prevention mechanism by Rubia cordifolia

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic procedure for the formation of plant extract.
Inhibition efficiency (a) of rubia cordifolia and corrosion rate(b) with and without KI.
Graph of Langmuir adsorption isotherm at varied concentrations in 3.5 % NaCl for steel.
Tafel Polarization curve in 3.5 % NaCl under varied concentrations of Rubia cordifoliain the absence (a) and presence (b) of KI.
Nyquist plots for steel in 3.5 % NaCl under various concentrations, when(a) KI is absent, and (b) KI is present.
Bode plot for steel in 3.5 % NaCl under varied concentrations, (a) with KI, (b) without KI.
UV absorbance measured before and after, with and without varied amounts of Rubia cordifolia.
SEM pictures of (a) polished steel (b) corroded steel dipped in 3.5 % NaCl (c) inhibited steel in presence of plant extract when KI is absent and (d) inhibited steel surface when KI is present.
Representation of mechanism of corrosion inhibition.
Efficacy of different plants in corrosion inhibition.
Inhibition efficiencies of Rubia cordifolia in 3.5 % NaCl.
Adsorption parameters of steel with and without KI.
Steel polarization behaviour studied at varied concentrations of Rubia cordifolia in the absence and presence of KI.
Impedance parameters of steel in 3.5 % NaCl under varied amounts of Rubia cordifolia when KI is absent and present.
Quantum chemical parameter values for various phytochemicals.
Optimized structure, HOMO and LUMO of various inhibitory molecules.

head.ce:title: Mixture of Rubia cordifolia and KI as an efficient corrosion inhibitor for steel in 3.5% NaCl: electrochemical and surface studies of steel

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study evaluates the corrosion inhibition performance of Rubia cordifolia extract on mild steel in a 3.5 % NaCl solution, with and without the addition of potassium iodide (SSKI oral solution). The extract contains active phytoconstituents such as alizarin, mollugin, naphthoquinone, ruberythric acid, saponins, and triterpenoids, which contribute to its anticorrosive behavior through adsorption on the steel surface. Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) results revealed that both anodic and cathodic reactions were effectively suppressed, achieving maximum inhibition efficiencies of 88 % without KI and 95.2 % with KI at 400 mg/L concentration. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) confirmed this trend, showing an increase in charge transfer resistance (Rct) from 15.78 Ω·cm² (blank) to 139.21 Ω·cm² (without KI) and 260.03 Ω·cm² (with KI), indicating the formation of a compact and stable protective film. UV–Visible analysis further validated the adsorption of phytochemical components on the steel surface, as reflected by decreased absorbance after corrosion testing. These results demonstrate that Rubia cordifolia extract acts as an efficient, eco-friendly corrosion inhibitor for steel in saline environments, and its synergistic combination with KI enhances inhibition performance through improved film stability and surface coverage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The use of steel is of great importance to offer sustainable development. The industry’s commitment to sustainability was significantly reaffirmed in the public eye when the steel and construction sector unveiled its sustainability strategy at the end of 2002. It is intended to ensure the industry’s continued health, allowing companies to make a profit while taking social and environmental concerns. It outlines how steel can be utilised to provide more environmentally friendly buildings at the stages of design, construction, use, and demolition [1]. Steel is a budget-friendly material that lasts longer and offers various stability levels. Steel offers many benefits for the automotive industry, such as easy shaping, consistent supply, and corrosion resistance while coated with zinc, simple joining, recyclability, and strong energy absorption in crashes. Steel is used in various vehicle parts, like the engine, chassis, wheels, and body, to make them lighter, improve fuel efficiency, and enhance strength, stiffness, and overall performance [2]. The growth of steel structures, particularly large space structures, long-span bridges, and super high-rise buildings, has led to the development of more advanced building material. By using high-strength steels, a structure can be made safer, more affordable, and more environmentally friendly by reducing the weight, seismic action, member size, welding effort, and amount of welding material [3].

Among the most used industrial materials are steel and its alloys. When these materials come into contact with saline conditions, they sustain significant corrosion damage. Sodium chloride is present in saline water. The ions released from NaCl help in the reduction of oxygen to form water. Steel can be shielded against corrosion attack using a variety of methods. These consist of surface coating, anodic and cathodic prevention, material alteration, environmental changes, and the implementation of corrosion inhibitors. While in some methodsthey put a persistent coating on the surface of metal, others rely on eliminating the moisture or oxygen. However, some methods work by transforming the anodic substance into a cathode [4–8].

The addition of KI significantly improved the inhibition performance of Rubia cordifolia extract. This enhancement can be attributed to the synergistic interaction between iodide ions and the phytochemical constituents of the extract. The iodide ions adsorb preferentially onto the mild steel surface due to their high polarizability, creating negatively charged sites that promote the electrostatic attraction and subsequent adsorption of positively charged or protonated inhibitor molecules. This cooperative adsorption mechanism increases the surface coverage and stabilizes the protective layer formed on the steel surface. As a result, both the corrosion current density and charge transfer are markedly reduced, while the inhibition efficiency rises [9]. Corrosion inhibitors are chemicals added to the environment that prevent rust to protect metals from getting damaged. They come in different types, each working in its own way [10]. Corrosion inhibitors, such as plant extracts, are praised for being environment-friendly and simple to obtain, creating them excellent alternatives for protecting [11]. The previous researchers worked on different green corrosion inhibitors, whose results are shown in Table 1 . This study investigates the effectiveness of Rubia cordifolia plant extract to slow down rusting in saline conditions, with the objective of exploring its practical uses [12,13].

In this study, Rubia cordifolia, commonly known as Manjhishtha in certain regions, is used as a corrosion inhibitor. This medicinal plant is employed to get rid ofcorrosion. The study investigates how an extract from the plantRubia cordifolia affects the rusting of steel in saline conditions, employing weight loss analysis and EIS.The presence of many bonds and different atoms in the phytochemical (as shown in Table 2 ) of plant extract is principally accountable for its anti-corrosive abilities [24].

This study investigates the ability of the Rubia cordifolia plant extract to prevent corrosion on steel. By conducting corrosion tests and surface analysis, this research explores its efficacy and inhibition processat various amounts. The main key goal is to enhance knowledge of rust prevention and promote the use of green, ecological inhibitors.
Rubia cordifolia has numerous bioactive components, including quinones, bicyclic hexapeptides, glycosides, saponins, triterpenoids, and ruberythric acid, found in medicinal herbs [25].

Despite the extensive research on plant-based corrosion inhibitors, the use of Rubia cordifolia extract for protecting mild steel in saline media has not been previously reported. Moreover, the synergistic effect of potassium iodide (KI) with the extract is systematically explored to enhance the inhibition efficiency and film stability. This combined approach has not been addressed in earlier literature for Rubia cordifolia. Comprehensive analyses, including weight loss measurements, potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), UV–Visible spectroscopy, and quantum chemical studies, are employed to elucidate the adsorption mechanism and inhibition behavior. Thus, this research contributes a new insight into the green corrosion inhibition mechanism of Rubia cordifolia, demonstrating its potential as a sustainable and effective alternative to conventional inhibitors in chloride-rich environments.

All experimental protocols involved mechanically cutting the steel alloy into a 1cm2 size. Each steel coupon’s surface was smoothed using a range of emery paper. The steel was initially washed with distilled water,then subsequently washed with ethanol.
The study utilised the test solution of 3.5 % NaCl. The electrochemical study and gravimetric study were performed using a 100 mL capacity of corrosive medium (3.5 % NaCl).

50 g of the samples were weighed. When adding powdered material, a cotton plug or a thimble of filter paper is inserted within the extraction chamber to keep the syphon tube from becoming clogged. The sample is placed inside the extraction chamber, and the Soxhlet apparatus has a condenser and a round-bottom flask, which is attached at the appropriate positions. The solvent is heated in a flask over a water bath to bring it to a boil. The vapours from the solvent travel via a vapour duct, condense, and fall as droplets inside the extraction chamber. Then collect the extract in a beaker and put it in the water bath until it dries.The weight of the obtained plant extract was 1.8gm which was used for further studies. The schematic diagram of the preparation of the extract is shown in Fig. 1 .

The inhibitor’s effectiveness is measured by the weight loss method, which records the substrate’s weight before and after exposure to the corrosive solution. The steel is first weighed using an electronic balance, then immersed in the solution. After the exposure period, the corroded steel is weighed again to determine weight loss. Corrosion resistance is evaluated by calculating the corrosion rate (CR) and inhibitor efficiency (ƞ %) [26,27]. The schematic diagram of the weight loss procedure is shown in Fig. 2 .
(1)
η
%
=
W ∘
−
W i
W ∘
×
100
Where, W0 = weight of steel after immersed in blank solution

Wi = weight of steel after immersed in different concentration
(2)
C R =
Δ
W
D × A × T
× K
Where, CR is Corrosion Rate
D = Density of Steel
A = Area of cross section of steel coupon
T = Corrosion time

The PGSTAT-204 lab equipment, set up with three cells connected to an electrochemical workstation, was used for electrochemical studies. This setup was made of a Pyrex glass flask with a flat bottom and three openings on each side for the working, reference, and counter electrodes. As reference and counter electrodes, platinum and silver wire were employed. In this setup, the reference electrode used is a saturated calomel electrode, the counter electrode consists of platinum, and theworking electrode is made of steel [28]. For the electrochemical measurements, 100 mL of aggressive medium is used. Steel is enclosed in the araldite resin with an observable area of 1 cm2. The enclosed steel is cleaned with different textures of sandpaper.
The current-potential plots were measured by slowly changing the electrode potential from −250 mV to +250 mV at a speed of 1 mV per second.

The efficiency was calculated by using the formula [29]:
(3)
η %
=
I
0 c o r r
−
I
i c o r r
I
0 c o r r
×
100
where
I
0 C o r r
and
I
i C o r r
are current densities in the presence and absence of extract respectively.

The electrochemical workstation and three-cell glass assembly, identical to those used for potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) analysis, were also utilised for EIS analysis. The inhibition efficiencies were calculated using the formula [30]:
(4)
η %
=
R
c t
−
R
0
ct
R P
×
100
Where R °ct and Rct depicts the charge transfer resistance in the absence and presence ofRubia cordifoliaplant extract, respectively.

The spectrophotometric method was performed on the prepared ordinary samples of steel dipped in 3.5 % NaCl in the presence and absence of potassium iodide (SSKI oral solution) for 24 h. There is a significant difference in the absorbance of the Ultraviolet spectra of the Rubia cordifolia before and after the prevention. Immersing steel into an inhibitor solution leads to the formation of ashielding layer onthe metal’s surface, showsthe adsorption of chemical constituents and acreating the bond withtheinhibitor molecule [31].

For the SEM study, the JSM IT500 instrument was employed. Micrographs were used to determine if the metal is protected because an organic layer forms on its surface [32]. The polished mild steel specimen is submerged in 100 ml of 3.5 % NaCl solution and 100 ml of inhibitor solution with optimal concentrations independently with the purpose of assess the changes in thetopography of the surface of the naked electrode and inhibitor film created an electrode. The metal specimens are submerged for three hours, then cleaned with double-distilled water and dried.This helps confirm if the inhibitor or KI has effectively bound to the surface. KI contributes to establishing a stable shielding layer, enhancing the performance of an inhibitor.

The performance of a corrosion inhibitor is largely determined by its molecular structure, which plays an essential role in how well it can prevent corrosion. One of the key factors in this process is the way the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) interacts with the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO)of the inhibitor molecules ofthe iron (Fe) surface [33]. This interaction helps the inhibitor stick to the metal surface, making it better at forming a protective layer. As a result, the inhibitor reduces the metal's susceptibility to corrosion, effectively hindering the electrochemical reactions that lead to degradation.A smaller change in energy values determines the optimal condition for the binding of solid inhibitory molecules onto a surface of metal [34].The energy difference between the orbitals can be calculated by using the formula 5–8 [35,36].
(5)
▵ E =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
Where ΔE = energy difference between HOMO and LUMO,
E(LUMO) = energy of LUMO and

E(HOMO) = energy of HOMO
(6)
χ =
−
E
L U M O
+
E
H O M O
2
( χ ) =
Electronegativity
(7)
Y =
E
L U M O
−
E
H O M O
2
(γ) = Global hardness
(8)
σ =
1 Y
(σ) = Chemical softness

Table 3 shows weight loss measurements of steel samples. Weight loss tests were performed at room temperature using Rubia cordifolia extract at different concentrations of 100 ppm, 200 ppm, 300 ppm, and 400 ppm, both in the presence and absence of KI. The weight loss data was used to check how well Rubia cordifolia extract prevents steel from corroding at different concentrations. The findings demonstrate that the inhibitory effectiveness improves as the concentration of Rubia cordifolia plant extract increases. The table and figure show that higher inhibitor concentrations improve inhibition efficiency. Here, we looked at how heteroatoms help inhibitors work better in corrosive environments by sticking to the steel surface and creating a protective shield on the surface of the metal. It is suggested that when the concentration of the extract increases, more phytochemicals become available, leading to greater adsorption. This happens because the heteroatoms in the inhibitor donate their lone pair of electrons to fill the empty d-orbitals on the metal’s interface. This means the inhibitor attaches better to the surface, lowering corrosion in extreme environments [37,38]. This combination of the inhibitor and KI showed better corrosion inhibition at all concentrations compared to the extract alone. The graph shown in Fig. 3 [(a) and (b)] illustrates the inhibitory efficacy and corrosion rate of Rubia cordifolia. Table 4 .

The study found that inhibitor molecules consistently attach to the steel surface, as shown by adsorption isotherms. Adding the inhibitor slows down corrosion and helps the adsorption process reach a stable state. Results from weight loss experiments were helpful in studying how the plant extract sticks to coated steel surfaces using the Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The Langmuir adsorption isotherm states that the adsorption energy stays the same regardless of how much of the steel’s surface is covered, and the adsorbed species do not affect each other. Langmuir adsorption isotherm can be expressed by the Eq. (8) [39] Fig. 4 :
(8′)
C
θ
=
1
K
a d s
+ C
Where C =concentration and

K
a d s
= constant of adsorption.

Tafel graphs representing polarization behaviour of steels at different concentrations of the plant extract, both with and without the addition of KI, are displayed in Fig 5 a and 5b Various potentiodynamic polarization parameters such as corrosion potential (Ecorr), cathodic (βc) and anodic (βa) Tafel slopes, and corrosion current density (Icorr) are determined by estimating the polarization curves. Both anodic and cathodic current densities are crucially reduces and the efficiency reaches 88 % and 95.2 % at 400 ppm. When the concentration of extract is increased, the current density reduces, demonstrating that the inhibition became more effective. Table 5 provides specific corrosion properties and the efficacy of Rubia cordifolia inhibition when KI is absent and in the presence of KI. The IE (ƞ %) was calculated by usingequation 3. Fig. 5ashows the polarization graphs of Tafel obtained in 3.5 % NaCl with different concentrations of Rubia cordifolia plant extract, and KI was absent. Fig. 5b shows the polarization graphs for Tafel analysis were achieved in 3.5 % NaCl (saline medium) with different concentrations of Rubia cordifolia plant extract, while in the presence of KI. The Potentiodynamic Polarization (PDP) analysis was conducted at a scan rate of 1 mV/s [40].The consistent drop in current density with increasing concentration of Rubia cordifolia extract indicates an enhanced inhibition performance due to the progressive adsorption of phytoconstituents on the mild steel surface. As the inhibitor concentration increases, more active sites on the metal surface become covered by adsorbed molecules, forming a protective barrier that limits both anodic metal dissolution and cathodic reduction reactions. This results in a uniform decrease in corrosion current density (Icorr) values across the polarization curves. The observed trend confirms that the inhibition efficiency is concentration-dependent, suggesting that Rubia cordifolia acts as a mixed-type inhibitor predominantly controlling the cathodic process through surface film formation.

Fig. 6 shows Nyquist plots and Fig. 7 shows Bode plots, illustrating the behaviour of the steel. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy(EIS) parameters for these plots are shown in Table 6 . The Nyquist plots show a clear pattern: when the concentration of the inhibitor increases, the size of semi – circle also gets bigger. This sequence is consistent with expected increase in an inhibitor’s inhibition capacity. When the amount of plant extract rises, the value of Rct also increases, showing enhanced adsorption of the extract. The protective film formed, making the protective layer resistance the main factor in rust prevention. The IE (ƞ %) is calculated by using the Eq. (4). The bode diagram illustrates that when a green inhibitor is added, a preventive coating develops on the metal surface, this makes the curves move to lower values of frequencies [41].

The UV spectrum of Rubia cordifolia was studied before and after the corrosion process. The aim of this spectral study is to verify whether the plant extract adheres to the steel. Fig. 8 presents the UV–visible spectra of the Rubia cordifolia extract solution at different concentrations with KI, both before and after immersing the steel in the solution for 24 h. The analysis reveals that, after 24 h of immersion, the steel dipped in the corrosive medium consisting of the inhibitor exhibits low absorbance as compared to steel in the corrosive medium without the inhibitor. Based on this observation, the coupons dipped in a corroding medium carrying the Rubia cordifolia extract form a shielding layer above the surface of the specimen as a result of the inhibitor's phytochemicals binding to its surface [42].

Figs. 9 (a), 8(b), 8(c), and 8(d) depict the surface morphology of steel specimens that were cleaned, inhibited in the corrosive solution (NaCl), without KI and with KI, respectively, both without and with the plant extract, after 24 h of immersion. Fig. 9(a) depicts the fine striations on the surface of steel prior to dipping, signifying a consistently even, refined, and uniform texture. In Fig. 9(b) severe surface irregularities were caused by corrosion in 3.5 % NaCl. However, in 3.5 % NaCl with the inhibitor, corrosion was mitigated, leading to the formation of a preventive layer [Fig. 9(c) absence of KI, Fig. 9(d) presence of KI].

A quantum chemical study shows a relationship between how an inhibitor works and how its molecule reacts. This is done by studying the electron-receiving and -giving properties of the molecule. A lower value of ΔE signifies more effective absorption onto the surface of the metal. Data shown in Table 7 show that Alizarin exhibits higher adsorption capacity as compare to Mollugin and Naphthoquinone, indicating a strong affinity for the metal surface.Table 8 shows the optimizing structure, HOMO and LUMO of the inhibitory molecule. This study identifies the optimizing structures of inhibitors such as Alizarin, Mollugin and Naphthoquinone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of the corrosion rate
ce:section.ce:para: Table 9 presents the corrosion rate values determined from both weight loss and potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements for mild steel in 3.5 % NaCl solution containing different concentrations of Rubia cordifolia extract, with and without KI. A consistent decrease in corrosion rate was observed with increasing inhibitor concentration for both methods. The presence of KI further enhanced the inhibition performance, as evidenced by a more pronounced reduction in corrosion rate. Although the absolute CR values obtained from weight loss are lower than those from PDP, both methods exhibit a similar trend, indicating strong correlation and consistency between the electrochemical and gravimetric techniques. This agreement validates the reliability of the inhibitor’s performance in minimizing steel degradation under saline conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Corrosion prevention mechanism by Rubia cordifolia
ce:section.ce:para: To acknowledge how plant extracts, prevent corrosion, we need to look at how the chemical constituents present in Rubia cordifolia stick to the metal surface. These chemical constituents bind to metallic interface, close the reacting sites, and prevent corrosion. The lone pair of the electron from the hetero atom helps the movement of electrons to metal from the inhibitor, creating a coordinate bond. The phytoconstituents present in Rubia cordifolia have numerous functional groups like carbonyl, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups. These elements significantly minimize the corrosion in 3.5 % NaCl(saline) medium [43,44]. Phytoconstituents containing carbonyl and hydroxyl groups can form coordinate bonds with ions present on the metal surface, like oxygen. The creation of coordination complexes as well protects the surface by keeping metal ions stable and blocking the reaction with aggressive medium. Phytochemicals prevent corrosion effectively through several processes, such as adsorption, forming a protective layer, transfer of electrons, and creating coordination complexes [45]. The diagrammatic presentation of the corrosion inhibition by the Rubio cordifolia is shown in Fig. 10 .

Future research can be directed toward integrating advanced computational techniques with experimental findings to gain a deeper understanding of the corrosion inhibition mechanism of Rubia cordifolia. Theoretical methods have proven effective in other studies for elucidating the adsorption orientation, electron density distribution, and interaction energy between inhibitor molecules and metallic surfaces. Comparing the results of Rubia cordifoliawith those of reported inhibitors [6,46,47] (e.g. Ageratum conyzoides, Alchornea cordifolia, Aspilia africana, Baphia nitida, ornidazole, and Millettia aboensis) through computational analysis can offer valuable insight into structure–activity relationships and adsorption behavior.

The present study demonstrates the effective corrosion inhibition behavior of Rubia cordifolia extract on mild steel in 3.5 % NaCl solution. The inhibition efficiency increased with higher inhibitor concentrations, achieving maximum protection at 400 ppm. The addition of KI further enhanced the inhibition performance, confirming a strong synergistic effect between the extract and iodide ions.

Weight loss and electrochemical (PDP and EIS) measurements showed consistent trends, indicating that the inhibitor significantly reduced corrosion rate and charge transfer at the metal–solution interface. Tafel polarization results suggested that the extract acted as a mixed-type inhibitor, controlling both anodic and cathodic reactions.

The adsorption of inhibitor molecules on the mild steel surface followed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm, suggesting monolayer adsorption through physical and chemical interactions. UV–Vis spectral analysis confirmed the formation of a complex between phytoconstituents and metal ions after immersion, supporting the protective film formation.

Quantum chemical calculations further corroborated the experimental results, showing that the active components of Rubia cordifolia possess high electron-donating ability, which facilitates adsorption onto the metal surface.
Overall, the findings establish Rubia cordifolia extract, particularly in the presence of KI, as an efficient, green, and sustainable corrosion inhibitor for mild steel in saline environments.
No Data associated in the manuscript.
Not applicable

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics, consent to participate, and consent to publish declarations
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

J. Construc. Steel Res

Mater, Today Sustain

Curr. Res. Green Sustain. Chem
